title: Pulicaria gnaphalodes
intro: Pulicaria gnaphalodes, also known as Egyptian fleabane, is a perennial herb belonging to the Asteraceae family, native to Africa and the Middle East. Traditionally, the plant has been used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, diarrhea, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antispasmodic properties. The bioactive constituents of Pulicaria gnaphalodes include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In herbal medicine, Pulicaria gnaphalodes is commonly prepared as a tea, infusion, or decoction, as well as being used in topical applications for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties.
taxonomy: Pulicaria gnaphalodes is classified as a species within the family Asteraceae and the genus Pulicaria. The genus Pulicaria belongs to the tribe Inuleae within the subfamily Asteroideae. This classification places Pulicaria gnaphalodes within the larger group of flowering plants known as angiosperms. Further, it can be classified as a member of the clade called Eudicots and the clade called Core eudicots. This classification reflects the evolutionary relationships between Pulicaria gnaphalodes and other plant species.
